questions.question: A urinary tract infection (UTI) can occur in the kidneys, ureters, bladder, or urethra.
Women get UTIs less frequently than men because their urethra is longer.
Elderly people in nursing homes are at higher risk for UTIs.
Pregnancy is NOT a risk factor for developing a UTI.
Immobility is a risk factor for urinary tract infection.
Hematuria (blood in the urine) is a symptom of UTI.
Nocturia refers to the absence of urination at night.
A urine culture is performed to identify the type of bacteria and choose the appropriate antibiotic.
A mild case of cystitis always requires antibiotic treatment.
Chronic or recurrent UTI may require antibiotic therapy for up to 6 months to 2 years.
Patients should wipe from back to front after toileting to prevent UTIs.
Urinating after sexual intercourse helps prevent UTIs.
Alcohol and caffeine are recommended fluids for patients with UTI.
Renal failure is defined as total or nearly total loss of kidney function.
There is only one type of renal failure.
Diabetic nephropathy is the most common/associated/chronic renal failure.
Hypertension nephrosclerosis is the leading cause of chronic renal failure.
Neurogenic bladder is caused by impaired nerve fibers from the bladder to the spinal cord.
Bladder outlet obstruction due to an enlarged prostate gland is a post-renal cause of CRF.
Neurogenic bladder is caused by impaired nerve fibers from the bladder to the spinal cord.
Poor cardiac function is a renal cause of chronic renal failure.
Kussmaul breathing in CRF patients is caused by metabolic alkalosis.
Pruritus (itching) in CRF patients is due to accumulation of urea frost on the skin.
Salt and water retention in CRF can lead to congestive heart failure and hypertension.
Hemodialysis works by circulating blood through a semi-permeable membrane (dialyzer) to remove toxins.
An AV fistula involves the surgical joining of an artery and vein under the skin.
Kidney stones in both ureters are a pre-renal cause of CRF.
Renal biopsy is one of the laboratory/diagnostic studies used to evaluate CRF.
Osteomalacia and renal osteodystrophy are skeletal complications of chronic renal failure.
Dialysis is only indicated when a kidney transplant is not possible.
A UTI is best defined as:

options.A: Inflammation of the kidney parenchyma only
options.B: An infection anywhere along the urinary tract including kidneys, ureters, bladder, or urethra
options.C: A bacterial infection limited to the urethra
options.D: A viral infection of the bladder only

questions.explanation: UTI may involve any part of the urinary tract.

questions.question: Which anatomical reason makes women more prone to UTIs than men?

options.B: Women have a longer urethra closer to the vagina
options.C: Women have a shorter urethra closer to the anus

questions.explanation: Shorter female urethra and proximity to the anus increase bacterial access.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a risk factor for UTI?
questions.explanation: Regular physical exercise is not listed as a UTI risk factor.

questions.question: Cloudy urine and foul odor are symptoms of:
questions.explanation: Cloudy, foul-smelling urine is a common UTI symptom.

questions.question: The diagnostic test that identifies the specific bacteria causing a UTI and guides antibiotic choice is:
Urinalysis in a patient with UTI typically reveals:

options.B: White blood cells or red blood cells

questions.question: A patient with chronic or recurrent UTI may require antibiotic therapy for:
Which instruction is MOST important to prevent UTIs in a female patient?
Which of the following fluids should be AVOIDED by a UTI patient?
Urinating after sexual intercourse is recommended because it helps:
Surgery for UTI is recommended when:

options.C: An anatomical abnormality is present causing the infection

questions.question: Wearing cloth (cotton) undergarments is recommended to prevent UTIs because:

options.A: Cotton is more absorbent of urine
options.B: Cotton allows better air circulation, reducing moisture and bacterial growth

questions.question: Renal failure is defined as:

options.C: Total or nearly total loss of kidney function

questions.question: How many types of renal failure are described?
Which classification includes poor cardiac function as a cause of CRF?
Neurogenic bladder causing CRF is classified as:
The MOST common cause of chronic renal failure is:
The SECOND leading cause of chronic renal failure is:
Polycystic kidney disease is characterized by:
Which gastrointestinal symptom is associated with chronic renal failure?
Rapid deep breathing (Kussmaul breathing) in CRF patients is caused by:
Pruritus (generalised itching) in CRF patients is caused by:

options.C: Accumulation of urea frost on the skin

questions.question: Which skin change is associated with CRF?

options.C: Yellow or brown skin discolouration with increased pigmentation

questions.question: Salt and water retention in CRF leads to all of the following EXCEPT:
Neurological manifestations of CRF include all of the following EXCEPT:
Which laboratory panel is used in the diagnostic evaluation of CRF?

options.B: Full electrolyte panel including calcium, phosphate, uric acid, magnesium, and albumin

questions.question: Which radiographic study evaluates for obstruction, stones, tumour, and kidney size in CRF?
The goal of conservative management in CRF is:

options.B: Retaining kidney function as much as possible

questions.question: Which medication may be used to treat anemia in CRF patients?
Dialysis is a process that:

options.B: Replaces only hormone functions of the kidney
options.C: Removes fluid, waste products, and toxic substances from blood when kidneys cannot work
options.D: Increases urine production in failing kidneys

questions.question: In hemodialysis, blood is circulated through:
Vascular access for hemodialysis can be achieved through:

options.B: An AV fistula or AV graft

questions.question: An AV fistula is created by:

options.A: Inserting a synthetic tube between artery and vein
options.B: Surgically joining an artery and vein under the skin
options.D: Placing a catheter in the subclavian vein

questions.question: The two replacement therapy options for lost kidney function are:
Atherosclerosis of the renal arteries causes CRF by:

options.A: Blocking urine outflow from the kidney
options.C: Inducing ischaemic nephropathy through reduced blood flow

questions.question: Bone disease in CRF (renal osteodystrophy) is caused by:

options.B: Impaired calcium and phosphate metabolism due to kidney failure

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes pressure in the lower pelvis as a symptom?

options.A: It is specific to chronic renal failure
options.B: It is a symptom of UTI
options.C: It only occurs in men with prostate disease
options.D: It is a neurological symptom of CRF

questions.question: Bowel incontinence is a risk factor for UTI because:

options.B: Faecal contamination increases bacterial access to the urethra

questions.question: Frequent and urgent need to urinate as a symptom of UTI is also called:
Which of the following is a pre-renal cause of chronic renal failure?

options.C: Chronic liver failure reducing renal perfusion

questions.question: Keeping the genital area clean is a preventive measure for:
Which finding on urinalysis indicates possible UTI?
Decreased fluid intake is a risk factor for UTI because:

options.A: It increases urine concentration, killing bacteria
options.B: It causes urinary stasis, allowing bacteria to multiply

questions.question: A patient with CRF has fatigue, headache, and a general feeling of illness. This is called:
Angiography or CT scan in CRF patients is used to evaluate:
In CRF management, which associated diseases must be controlled first?

options.B: Hypertension, congestive heart failure, UTIs, and kidney stones

questions.question: Decreased sensation in hands, feet, or other areas in CRF is a manifestation of:
The purpose of erythropoietin injection in CRF management is to:

options.B: Stimulate red blood cell production and control anemia

questions.question: Which preventive measure helps reduce bacterial growth by maintaining a dry perineal environment?
Coma in a patient with CRF is classified as a:
Which of the following correctly states the relationship between diabetes and CRF?

options.B: Diabetic nephropathy is the most common cause of CRF

questions.question: Ascites in CRF patients results from:

options.B: Salt and water retention causing fluid accumulation in the peritoneum

questions.question: Nail abnormalities in CRF patients are classified as:
The purpose of renal ultrasound in CRF diagnosis is to:

options.B: Evaluate for obstruction, stones, tumour, kidney size, and chronic change

questions.question: Which of the following is a correct prevention strategy for UTIs?

options.A: Drink plenty of fluids to flush bacteria
options.B: Reduce fluid intake to rest the bladder
options.C: Use synthetic undergarments for better protection
options.D: Urinate less frequently to retain bladder capacity

questions.question: Hiccups in a patient with CRF are classified as:
Pain or burning with urination is medically termed:
The process by which blood is cleaned in hemodialysis uses the principle of:
Which of the following conditions is associated with an increased risk of UTI due to urinary stasis?
Dietary intervention is part of which aspect of CRF management?
UTI a. Swelling caused by fluid retention b
Cystitis b. Infection in any part of urinary tract c
Hematuria c. Inflammation of the bladder d
Nocturia d. Presence of blood in urine e
Edema e. Frequent urination at night a
Renal failure f. Loss of kidney function f
Hemodialysis g. Artificial cleansing of blood g
Anuria h. Absence of urine output h
Oliguria i. Decreased urine output i
Polyuria j. Excessive urine output j
Pruritus k. Itching sensation k
Dysuria l. Painful urination l
Dyspnea m. Difficulty breathing m
Fatigue n. Extreme tiredness n
Nausea o. Feeling of wanting to vomit o
Which symptom in CRF results from the kidney's inability to excrete hydrogen
questions.answer_text: ions? A. Hypertension B. Edema C. Kussmaul breathing due to metabolic acidosis D. Pruritus Kidney cannot excrete hydrogen ions. Metabolic acidosis develops. Result: Kussmaul breathing.

questions.question: Define urinary tract infection (UTI) and describe its causes, risk factors, and
questions.answer_text: symptoms in detail. UTI = infection anywhere in urinary tract. Usually bacterial ascent, often E. coli. Risk factors: female anatomy, pregnancy, catheterization, stones, immobility, low fluids. Symptoms: dysuria, urgency, frequency, hematuria, cloudy urine, pelvic pain, fever.

questions.question: Outline the nursing education that should be provided to a patient to prevent
questions.answer_text: recurrent urinary tract infections. Drink plenty of fluids and void regularly. Wipe front to back; keep perineum clean and dry. Void after intercourse. Avoid caffeine/alcohol; wear cotton underwear.

questions.question: Classify the causes of chronic renal failure into pre-renal, renal, and post-
questions.answer_text: renal, and give two examples of each. Pre-renal: poor cardiac function, chronic liver failure. Renal: diabetic nephropathy, HTN nephrosclerosis. Post-renal: neurogenic bladder, bilateral obstruction.

questions.question: Describe the signs and symptoms of chronic renal failure, organising them by
questions.answer_text: body system. GI: anorexia, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, hiccups. Respiratory: Kussmaul breathing. Skin: pruritus, discoloration, nail changes. CV/fluid: edema, hypertension, CHF, ascites. Neuro: fatigue, confusion, cramps, seizures, coma.

questions.question: Describe the conservative management of chronic renal failure and explain the
questions.answer_text: role of each component. Control underlying causes and preserve remaining renal function. Diet/fluid management and lab monitoring. Treat anemia, electrolytes, acidosis, bone disease. Prepare for replacement therapy when needed.

questions.question: Define hemodialysis and describe how it is performed, including vascular
questions.answer_text: access methods. Hemodialysis = artificial cleansing of blood. Blood passes through dialyzer membrane. Waste and extra fluid removed. Access via AV fistula/graft/catheter.

questions.question: Compare and contrast acute renal failure and chronic renal failure.
questions.answer_text: Acute renal failure: sudden, often reversible. Chronic renal failure: gradual, progressive, usually irreversible. Both may need dialysis.

questions.question: What are the dietary restrictions recommended for patients with chronic renal
questions.answer_text: failure? Explain the rationale for each. Restrict sodium and fluid if overloaded. Restrict potassium and phosphorus. Moderate protein as ordered. Support calcium/vitamin D for bone health.

questions.question: Write a comprehensive nursing care plan for a patient with chronic renal
questions.answer_text: failure including nursing diagnoses, goals, and interventions. Diagnoses: excess fluid volume, poor nutrition, activity intolerance, electrolyte imbalance risk. Monitor I&O, weight, edema, BP, respiratory status, labs. Provide diet, meds, skin care, education, and dialysis teaching. Source note: MCQ 31, 33, and 34 in the original file appear malformed; answers use the best-fit intended meaning.

questions.question: Pneumonia is inflammation of the airway and alveoli caused by infectious agents.
Inhalation pneumonia can be caused by gastric content.
Smoking is not considered a predisposing factor for pneumonia.
A depressed cough reflex increases the risk of pneumonia.
Sudden onset and shaking chills are signs of pneumonia.
Chest pain in pneumonia is usually relieved by coughing.
Tachypnea in pneumonia may be accompanied by nasal flaring.
Sputum culture is used as a diagnostic tool for pneumonia.
Frequent turning of bedridden patients helps prevent pneumonia.
Isolation of the patient is never necessary during the acute stage of pneumonia.
An upright position helps obtain greater lung expansion in pneumonia patients.
Warm fluid intake should be discouraged for pneumonia patients.
Postural drainage is a nursing management technique for pneumonia.
Hypotension and shock are potential complications of pneumonia.
Atelectasis in pneumonia is caused by accumulated secretions.
Pulmonary TB is a communicable bacterial infection.
TB is caused by the Mycobacterium tuberculosis bacteria.
TB can only affect the lungs and no other organs.
Primary stage TB infection is usually asymptomatic.
Drug-resistant bacteria can emerge from incomplete TB treatment.
Close contact with an active TB patient is a risk factor.
Children under age 15 are not at risk for TB.
Night sweats and weight loss are systemic symptoms of TB.
Hemoptysis means coughing up blood.
TB is spread through aerosol droplets from coughing or sneezing.
Latent TB patients can transmit the disease to others.
A tuberculin skin test is used in TB diagnosis.
Rifampicin and Isoniazid are common antibiotics for TB.
There is a reliable TB vaccine available for all adults.
Hospitalised TB patients should be kept in negative pressure rooms.
COPD airflow limitation is fully reversible.
Emphysema and chronic bronchitis are types of COPD.
Genetic deficiency of alpha antitrypsin is a risk factor for COPD.
Tobacco smoke is a primary risk factor for COPD.
Tachypnea is a clinical manifestation of COPD.
In COPD, breathing out takes a shorter time than breathing in.
Pursed-lip breathing is a sign/technique used by COPD patients.
Finger clubbing can be a sign of emphysema.
Quitting smoking is a medical management strategy for COPD.
Bronchodilators are used to dilate the airways.
Patients with COPD should avoid dust-free environments.
High fluid intake (2–3 litres) is recommended for COPD patients.
Dairy products should be avoided if they increase sputum production.
Tachycardia is a potential adverse effect of bronchodilators.
Right-sided heart failure is a complication of COPD.
Which type of pneumonia is caused by inhaling gastric content?
Which of the following is a predisposing factor for pneumonia?
A common sign of pneumonia is sputum that is:
Diagnostic tests for pneumonia include:
To prevent aspiration in unconscious patients, the nurse should:
What is the priority nursing action for a pneumonia patient with inadequate gas exchange?

Which of these helps loosen secretions in pneumonia?

Which antibiotic is commonly used to treat TB?
To promote airway clearance in TB, the nurse should encourage:

A complete TB evaluation includes:

A complication of TB is involvement of the:
COPD is characterized by:

Pulmonary rehabilitation involves:
To clear secretions in COPD, the nurse uses:
Nebulized saline inhalations help to:
COPD patients should monitor for tachycardia when taking:
A technique to manage fatigue in COPD is:
A sign of emphysema is:
COPD can lead to which skeletal issue?
In COPD, wheezing is heard through a:
Depression is a possible ______ of COPD.
Pneumonia (bacterial) A. Mycobacterium tuberculosis C
Pulmonary Tuberculosis B. Aspiration of gastric content A
Fungal Pneumonia C. Streptococcus bacteria E
Inhalation Pneumonia D. Long-term tobacco smoke exposure B
COPD E. Candida or Aspergillus species D
Rifampicin and Isoniazid A. Dilate airways and improve airflow in COPD D
Bronchodilators B. Relieve rhinorrhoea and sneezing in pneumonia A
Antihistaminics C. Protect high-risk persons from pneumonia B
BCG Vaccine D. Kill Mycobacterium tuberculosis E
Pneumococcal Vaccine E. Protect children against tuberculosis C
Define Pneumonia and list its four classifications.
questions.answer_text: Pneumonia = inflammation/infection of lung parenchyma and alveoli. Classifications: bacterial, viral, fungal, aspiration/inhalation.

questions.question: List five predisposing factors for pneumonia.
questions.answer_text: Smoking. Immunosuppression/chronic illness. Depressed cough reflex. Bed rest/immobility. Aspiration risk.

questions.question: What are the preventive measures for pneumonia?
questions.answer_text: Immunization and hand hygiene. Frequent turning, coughing, deep breathing, early ambulation. Oral care and aspiration precautions. Smoking cessation and hydration.

questions.question: Explain how Pulmonary Tuberculosis is transmitted.
questions.answer_text: TB spreads through airborne droplet nuclei. Released by coughing, sneezing, talking, singing. Others inhale droplets into lungs. Risk rises with crowding and poor ventilation.

questions.question: Identify five risk factors for developing Tuberculosis.
questions.answer_text: Close contact with active TB case. HIV/immunosuppression. Malnutrition. Overcrowding/poor ventilation. Diabetes/chronic illness/extremes of age.

questions.question: What are the systemic symptoms and common diagnostic tests for TB?
questions.answer_text: Symptoms: fever, night sweats, weight loss, fatigue, anorexia. Tests: TST/IGRA, sputum AFB/culture, chest X-ray, molecular testing.

questions.question: Define COPD and list its primary risk factors.
questions.answer_text: COPD = chronic progressive airflow limitation not fully reversible. Includes chronic bronchitis and emphysema. Main risks: smoking, biomass smoke, air pollution, dust/fumes, alpha-1 deficiency.

questions.question: Describe the nursing interventions to improve airway clearance in a COPD
questions.answer_text: patient. Assess breath sounds, sputum, RR, SpO2. High Fowler’s; pursed-lip and huff coughing. 2–3 L/day fluids if allowed. Bronchodilators/nebulization; chest physiotherapy/postural drainage.

questions.question: List five potential complications of COPD.
questions.answer_text: Respiratory failure. Recurrent infection/pneumonia. Pulmonary hypertension. Cor pulmonale/right heart failure. Depression, malnutrition, muscle dysfunction.

questions.question: Write a nursing care plan for a COPD patient including diagnoses, goals, and
questions.answer_text: interventions. Diagnoses: ineffective airway clearance, impaired gas exchange, activity intolerance. Goals: maintain airway, improve oxygenation, reduce dyspnea. Interventions: upright position, monitor ABGs/SpO2, airway clearance, oxygen and meds, smoking cessation teaching.

questions.question: Peptic ulcers can form in the esophagus.
Gastric ulcers are most commonly found in the greater curvature of the stomach.
H. pylori infection plays a role in the development of gastric ulcers.
Duodenal ulcers are often associated with protein-rich meals.
Stress ulcers usually occur after a chronic illness rather than an acute crisis.
In gastric ulcers, acid secretion is typically hyper-secretion.
Pain from a duodenal ulcer usually occurs 2–4 hours after meals.
Food typically aggravates the pain in a gastric ulcer.
Vomiting is a common symptom of duodenal ulcers.
Duodenal ulcers are more likely to manifest as haematemesis than gastric ulcers.
Melaena is more common with duodenal ulcers.
Fibre optic endoscopes allow direct visualisation of the stomach and duodenum.
Patients should limit fluids for 48 hours after a barium X-ray.
Barium may cause the stool to appear white for several days.
Haemorrhage is a potential complication of peptic ulcers.
Orthostatic hypotension is a manifestation of ulcer haemorrhage.
"Coffee ground" vomitus is more likely associated with a gastric ulcer.
A saline lavage via NGT is used during the management of haemorrhage.
Perforation is when GI contents empty into the peritoneal cavity.
The pain from perforation is described as dull and localised.
A rigid, tender abdomen is a sign of perforation.
Perforation is considered a surgical emergency.
Pyloric obstruction is caused by excessive acid only.
Persistent vomiting is a symptom of pyloric obstruction.
Pyloric obstruction can lead to metabolic acidosis.
Conservative therapy for ulcers includes the elimination of smoking.
Antibiotics are used to eradicate H. pylori infections.
Recurrence of ulcers is much higher (75–90%) if H. pylori is not treated.
Antacids are the initial drugs of choice for medical management.
Histamine H2 receptor antagonists increase HCl secretion.
Anticholinergic drugs speed up gastric motility.
Gastroduodenostomy involves connecting the stomach to the jejunum.
Gastrojejunostomy is the removal of the lower stomach and connecting it to the jejunum.
Total gastrectomy involves the removal of the entire stomach.
In a total gastrectomy, the esophagus is connected directly to the jejunum.
Subtotal gastrectomy involves removing the distal third of the stomach.
Hypokalaemia is a risk in patients with pyloric obstruction.
The "back diffusion" of HCl damages the gastric epithelium.
Bile reflux from the duodenum cannot damage the gastric mucosal barrier.
Duodenal ulcers lead to a large acid load in the duodenum.
Ischaemia is a contributing factor to the evolution of stress ulcers.
Upper GI series uses barium to detect structural disorders.
NGT suction helps prevent distension during haemorrhage management.
Perforation management includes keeping the patient NPO (nothing by mouth).
Stomach emptying is enhanced in pyloric obstruction.
Which of the following is NOT a location for a peptic ulcer?
Gastric ulcers are most common near the:
Which bacteria is heavily linked to peptic ulcers?
What is the primary cause of duodenal ulcers?
Stress ulcers are most common in which parts of the GI tract?
Pain that occurs 1-2 hours after a meal is characteristic of:

Hematemesis is more likely to occur with which type of ulcer?
Melena refers to:
The procedure used to visualize the esophagus, stomach, and duodenum directly is:
Following an Upper GI series, why is a mild laxative sometimes recommended?

options.B: If constipation occurs due to barium

questions.question: A "coffee ground" appearance of vomitus indicates:

options.B: Older blood affected by stomach acid

questions.question: Signs of orthostatic hypotension during hemorrhage include:

options.B: Drop in BP and dizziness upon standing

questions.question: Management of hemorrhage includes all the following EXCEPT:

The primary goal of using antibiotics in ulcer treatment is:

Which medication type stops cholinergic stimulation of HCl?
Gastroduodenostomy connects the remaining stomach to the:
Subtotal gastrectomy removes how much of the stomach?
Which surgical procedure connects the esophagus to the jejunum?
Back diffusion refers to HCl damaging the:
Protein-rich meals contribute to duodenal ulcers because they:
Which ulcer type is most associated with rapid emptying of food?
Ischemia is a major factor in:
In an Upper GI series, the patient ingests:
Clinical manifestations of hemorrhage include:
The purpose of NGT suction in hemorrhage is to:

options.C: Prevent distension and assess bleeding rate

questions.question: Perforation pain can spread to:
Which is NOT a management step for perforation?
Pyloric obstruction is caused by:
Abdominal bloating is a symptom of:
Histamine H2 receptor antagonists work by:

options.B: Blocking the activator of HCl secretion

questions.question: Gastrojejunostomy is also known as a procedure that reconnects the:
Dietary modifications are part of:
The recurrence rate of ulcers with H. pylori infection is:
Total gastrectomy is the:
Management of hemorrhage requires replacing:
Bright red hematemesis is more likely in:
Peptic ulcers are essentially:
Gastric Ulcer A. Manifests as rigid, tender abdomen and sudden sharp pain C
Duodenal Ulcer B. Involves reconnecting stomach to jejunum after removing part D
Perforation C. Characterised by pain 1–2 hours after meals; vomiting common A
Pyloric Obstruction D. Pain often relieved by food; may be relieved by food E
Gastrojejunostomy E. Leads to abdominal bloating, persistent vomiting, hypokalaemia B
Stress Ulcer C. Occurs after acute medical crisis, trauma, or major surgery C
Upper GI Series E. Diagnostic test involving barium ingestion; may cause white stools E
Haematemesis D. Bloody or coffee-ground vomit — more common with gastric ulcers D
H. pylori C. Bacterium — major cause of ulcer recurrence C
H2 Receptor Antagonists D. Block final intracellular activator of HCl secretion D
Define peptic ulcer and list the three main types.
questions.answer_text: Peptic ulcer = mucosal erosion from acid-pepsin injury. Main types: gastric, duodenal, stress ulcers. May also occur in lower esophagus.

questions.question: Compare the timing and nature of pain between Gastric and Duodenal ulcers.
questions.answer_text: Gastric ulcer pain: 1–2 h after meals and worsens with food. Duodenal pain: 2–4 h after meals/night and relieved by food or antacids.

questions.question: Explain the role of H. pylori in the development of peptic ulcers.
questions.answer_text: H. pylori inflames gastric mucosa. Weakens protective barrier. Increases susceptibility to acid-pepsin injury. Raises recurrence if untreated.

questions.question: What is the "Upper GI series" and what are the nursing responsibilities after
questions.answer_text: the procedure? Upper GI series = barium X-ray of esophagus/stomach/duodenum. Encourage fluids after test. Laxative may be needed. Pale stools may occur briefly.

questions.question: Describe the clinical manifestations of ulcer perforation.
questions.answer_text: Sudden severe sharp epigastric pain. Rigid board-like abdomen and tenderness. Absent bowel sounds, tachycardia, shock/peritonitis signs.

questions.question: Outline the management steps for a patient experiencing a GI haemorrhage.
questions.answer_text: NPO and IV access. Replace blood, fluids, electrolytes. Insert NGT if ordered. Monitor vitals, urine, bleeding. Prepare for endoscopy/surgery if uncontrolled.

questions.question: What causes pyloric obstruction and what are its symptoms?
questions.answer_text: Caused by edema, spasm, scarring near pylorus. Delays gastric emptying. Symptoms: fullness, bloating, vomiting, dehydration, weight loss, hypokalemia.

questions.question: Compare conservative and surgical management of peptic ulcers.
questions.answer_text: Conservative: rest, smoking cessation, avoid NSAIDs/irritants, diet changes. Medical: antacids, H2 blockers/PPIs, H. pylori therapy. Surgical: for perforation, obstruction, bleeding, refractory disease.

questions.question: Describe the types of surgical procedures used for peptic ulcers.
questions.answer_text: Billroth I: stomach remnant to duodenum. Billroth II: stomach remnant to jejunum. Subtotal gastrectomy: distal diseased stomach removed. Total gastrectomy: entire stomach removed; esophagus to jejunum.

questions.question: Write a nursing care plan for a patient with peptic ulcer including diagnoses,
questions.answer_text: goals, and interventions. Diagnoses: acute pain, fluid deficit, poor nutrition, anxiety. Assess pain, bleeding, abdomen, vitals, stools/vomitus. Give meds, IV/NGT/NPO care as indicated. Teach diet, smoking cessation, adherence, warning signs. Source note: a few MCQs and matching entries in Chapter 3 are flawed or duplicated in the original source, so answers use the closest intended meaning.

questions.question: Angina is defined as irreversible necrosis of heart muscle secondary to prolonged ischaemia.
Physical exertion can precipitate an angina attack by increasing myocardial oxygen demands.
Angina pain typically radiates to the neck, jaw, shoulders, and inner aspects of the upper extremities.
Classic angina is characterised by increasing frequency and duration of symptoms over time.
Nocturnal angina occurs only at night and is always related to a recumbent position.
Angina decubitus is discomfort precipitated by recumbent positions and relieved by sitting or standing.
During an acute angina attack, the patient's vital signs are always normal.
An exercise stress test involves performing an ECG while the patient exercises on a treadmill or stationary bicycle.
Chest X-ray findings are typically abnormal in patients with stable angina.
Nitroglycerin tablets should be stored in a light-coloured plastic bottle.
Cotton should be placed in the nitroglycerin bottle to protect the tablets.
If angina pain is not relieved after the first nitroglycerin tablet, a second tablet can be taken 3 to 5 minutes later.
Patients with angina should swallow their saliva while waiting for a sublingual nitroglycerin tablet to dissolve.
Headache is a known side effect of nitroglycerin.
Patients with angina should exercise vigorously immediately after meals.
Acute Myocardial Infarction (MI) is defined as irreversible necrosis of heart muscle secondary to prolonged ischaemia.
Hyperlipidaemia is one of the six primary risk factors for atherosclerotic coronary artery disease and MI.
Diaphoresis and dyspnea are common clinical manifestations of acute MI.
In acute MI, serum CPK levels typically decrease.
The initial ECG evaluation in an MI patient should be completed within 10 minutes of arrival.
Aspirin 160–325 mg chewed is part of initial emergency management of acute MI.
Sublingual TNG is contraindicated in MI when systolic blood pressure is below 90 mmHg.
Rehabilitation after MI aims to keep the patient as inactive as possible.
One sub-goal of cardiac rehabilitation is to help the client accept the limitations imposed by illness.
Occupational adjustment is NOT considered part of cardiac rehabilitation goals.
Eating heavy meals can precipitate angina by increasing demands on the heart.
Stress and emotionally provoking situations can precipitate angina by releasing adrenaline.
Exposure to cold is NOT considered a precipitating factor for angina.
Unstable angina shows a progressive pattern of increasing frequency, intensity, and duration of symptoms.
The nursing diagnosis 'anxiety related to fear of death' is appropriate for a patient with angina.
Angina is best described as:

options.B: A clinical syndrome characterised by paroxysmal chest pain or pressure
options.D: Reduced blood flow to the brain

questions.question: Which of the following is a precipitating factor for angina?
The type of angina that occurs with recumbent position and is relieved by sitting is:

questions.question: Acute Myocardial Infarction (MI) is caused by:

options.C: Irreversible necrosis of heart muscle secondary to prolonged ischemia

options.B: Fainting or near fainting without other cause

options.B: Helping the client live as full, vital, and productive a life as possible

options.A: Q waves and ST elevation permanently

options.B: To prevent further episodes and improve coping

options.B: Prevention and controlling of chest pain

options.B: Changing psychosocial factors affecting recovery from CAD

questions.question: The term preinfarction angina refers to:

options.B: Agonizing pain with severe apprehension and feeling of impending death
options.C: A dull ache in the lower back

options.B: Take prophylactic sublingual nitroglycerin before the activity

options.B: Cold wind causes vasoconstriction and increases cardiac workload

questions.question: When teaching about nitroglycerin, the nurse should emphasize that the tablet works by:

options.B: Causing vasodilation to reduce myocardial ischemia

options.A: It determines the patient's pain tolerance
options.B: It helps characterize the nature and pattern of angina for diagnosis and management
options.C: It assesses the patient's education level

questions.question: Which statement about Type A personality and angina is correct?

options.A: Type A personality is protective against angina
options.B: Type A personality is listed as a risk factor for angina
options.C: Personality type has no relation to heart disease
options.D: Only Type B personality is at risk

questions.question: The patient teaching about activity in angina should emphasize:

options.B: Moderate daily exercise that does not precipitate pain, with rest periods

questions.question: In a patient with acute MI, impairment of cognitive function without another cause is:

Classic Angina A. Irreversible necrosis of heart muscle — prolonged ischaemia D
Unstable Angina B. Angina occurring only at night E
Nocturnal Angina C. Discomfort precipitated by recumbent positions B
Angina Decubitus D. Short duration pain by exertion, relieved by rest C
Myocardial Infarction E. Progressive pattern of increasing intensity and duration A
Relief of Angina pain A. Usually severe and may be silent B
Severity of MI pain B. Relieved by rest and nitrates A
Common MI symptoms C. Physical exertion, cold exposure, or heavy meals E

MI Diagnostic Lab Study E. Nausea and vomiting D
Nitroglycerin storage Keep in dark-coloured glass bottle Keep in dark-coloured glass bottle
Sublingual NTG use Place under tongue; do not swallow saliva until dissolved Under tongue; do not swallow saliva
Diet education for Angina Low fat, small frequent meals; avoid caffeine Low-fat diet, small frequent meals, avoid caffeine
Cold weather precaution Cover mouth and nose with a scarf Cover mouth and nose with scarf
Initial MI emergency care Obtain ECG in less than 10 minutes ECG in less than 10 minutes
Define angina pectoris and describe its four main types with their
questions.answer_text: distinguishing characteristics. Angina = transient myocardial ischemia causing chest pain without necrosis. Types: classic/stable, unstable, nocturnal, decubitus. Differentiate by trigger, timing, and relief.

questions.question: Describe the nursing management of a patient with angina, including
questions.answer_text: assessment, nursing diagnoses, and interventions. Assess pain, triggers, duration, vitals, ECG, response to NTG. Nursing diagnoses: pain, anxiety, activity intolerance, knowledge deficit. Rest, oxygen/NTG as ordered, monitor rhythm/BP, reduce anxiety, teach prevention.

questions.question: Explain the health education that should be provided to a patient with angina
questions.answer_text: regarding medications, diet, activity, and cold weather. Store NTG correctly and use sublingually. May take before known triggers. Eat low-fat small frequent meals, avoid heavy meals/caffeine. Balance activity with rest; avoid exertion in cold weather and after meals.

questions.question: Outline the initial emergency management steps for a patient presenting with
questions.answer_text: acute myocardial infarction. Rapid assessment, IV access, monitor, ECG within 10 minutes. Give oxygen, chewed aspirin, NTG if appropriate. Give analgesia and obtain cardiac enzymes/urgent review.

questions.question: Describe the six sub-goals of cardiac rehabilitation after myocardial infarction
questions.answer_text: and explain the importance of each. Restore physical function with graded activity. Support illness acceptance and emotional coping. Improve social/family/sexual adjustment. Help return to work and reinforce long-term self-care.

questions.question: List and describe the six primary risk factors for myocardial infarction.
questions.answer_text: Hyperlipidemia. Hypertension. Tobacco use. Diabetes mellitus. Obesity. Male gender/family predisposition.

questions.question: Compare and contrast angina pectoris and myocardial infarction in terms of
questions.answer_text: definition, clinical presentation, and management. Angina = transient ischemia without necrosis. MI = prolonged ischemia with necrosis. MI pain is more severe/persistent and raises enzymes. MI needs emergency treatment; angina focuses on prevention and symptom relief.

questions.question: Write a comprehensive nursing care plan for a patient with acute myocardial
questions.answer_text: infarction including nursing diagnoses, goals, and interventions. Diagnoses: acute pain, decreased cardiac output, anxiety, activity intolerance. Goals: relieve pain, improve perfusion, prevent complications. Bed rest, oxygen, ECG/vital monitoring, IV access, give ordered drugs. Monitor for arrhythmias, hypotension, heart failure, low urine output. Begin rehab education and risk reduction. Source note: several Chapter 4 MCQs are malformed or mismatched in the original source; best-fit answers are used.
